text: The use of electrocatalysis for the production of commodity chemicals has been demonstrated as an opportunity to leverage intermittent renewable energy for on-site and on-demand production, enhancing grid reliability and reducing complex logistics associated with supply chains. Here, we investigate the ability to couple CO2 reduction (CO2RR) with nitrate reduction (NO3RR) using a molecular copper boron-imidazolate cage material denoted as BIF-29(Cu). Urea production was achieved at an average selectivity of 68.5% and current density of 424 μA cm-2. We show that BIF-29(Cu) is one of the most selective materials reported for multistep C-N bond forming reactions, even though it only contains isolated single-metal sites. Insights into the C-N bond forming mechanism were obtained using a suite of techniques that included electrochemical measurements, in-situ vibrational spectroscopy and atomic-scale simulations. These studies indicated that NO3RR and CO2RR occurred at isolated copper sites, while the most favorable pathway for the C-N bond forming reaction occurs between *CO and *NH2OH to produce urea. Overall, our results suggest that the use of supramolecular metal-organic cages can provide a materials strategy to achieve efficient catalysts containing atomically discrete active sites.
